text: I'm Christopher Waters, and this is my video on robotic distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy. I'm a surgeon in the Division of Advanced Gastrointestinal and Oncologic Surgery at Duke University. The patient for presentation is an obese, forty-two-year-old woman in good health previously, presented with an eighteen-month history of upper abdominal discomfort. She also complained of associated bloating and early satiety and had lost twelve pounds over a three-month period. Initial evaluation included upper and lower endoscopy, which were unrevealing, followed by imaging studies, which demonstrated a two point five centimeter mass in the tail of the pancreas. An attempted ultrasound was performed, but was poorly tolerated, although a final aspirate was obtained, revealing he had no carcinoma. The MRI demonstrates the mass. I recommended a robotic distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy.

The patient is positioned supine, then moved to reverse Trendelenburg with the left side elevated at a fifteen-degree angle. After diagnostic laparoscopy confirms no metastatic disease, the surgeon docks the robot and begins the procedure by dividing the gastrocolic ligament with a harmonic scalpel, inferior to the gastroepiploic vessels. The body of the pancreas becomes visible through the created window.

The surgeon observes the remarkably large splenic artery, measuring approximately twelve millimeters in diameter, pulsating visibly in the foreground. The artery follows a serpentine course. The dissection along the greater curvature is continued to the angle of His, completing the mobilization.

The surgeon reflects the stomach cephalad to achieve excellent exposure. The peritoneum is incised along the inferior margin of the pancreas, and a medial to lateral dissection is performed. The omentum and splenocolic ligament are divided, fully mobilizing the splenic flexure of the colon.

The surgeon uses hook cautery to divide the peritoneum at the superior margin of the pancreas, exposing the splenic artery. The splenic artery becomes visible as the surgeon moves under the inferior margin of the pancreas, elevating it from the retroperitoneum to reveal the splenic vein.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to perform fine dissection, separating the splenic vein from the pancreas. The dissection is carried underneath the splenic vein and taken to the splenic artery, with the splenic vein now encircled and the final dissection of the splenic artery underway.

The surgeon retracts the splenic artery cephalad, revealing the dorsal pancreatic artery branching off inferiorly. Dissection proceeds to the takeoff from the celiac axis. The surgeon navigates underneath the neck of the pancreas, threading a vessel loop through the passageway in preparation for division. Despite the pancreas being thick, attempts to divide it with a stapler are unsuccessful, leading to the decision to use energy for division.

The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the pancreatic duct. A small arterial bleeder is controlled using bipolar cautery. The pancreatic duct is identified and suture ligated with a 3-0 silk suture, then divided using a harmonic scalpel, exposing the splenic vessels.

The surgeon observes that the artery is larger than the vein and proceeds to divide the artery using a tan load of staples, followed by the division of the vein with the same staple load. The retroperitoneal dissection begins, elevating the pancreas out of the retroperitoneum. Adhesions are noted behind the tumor, and a portion of the transverse mesocolon is taken with the specimen.
The surgeon begins to maneuver past the tumor, preparing to take down the peritoneal reflection.

For simplicity, I generally dock the robot directly from the head for all the upper abdominal cases. You certainly could have come over the left shoulder for this case, but this creates some challenge at the extreme superior aspect of the dissection. But as you can see, this is very straightforward still.

The surgeon completes the dissection by taking down the remaining peritoneal reflection supporting the spleen, freeing the specimen. The pancreatic bed is irrigated and inspected for hemostasis. Three polypropylene mattress sutures are placed across the pancreas, and a drain is left along the pancreatic bed. The specimen is placed in a large container for retrieval via the supraumbilical site.

Her post-operative course was quite benign and her diet was readily advanced. By post-operative day five, she was tolerating a regular diet and had spontaneous bowel activity and was discharged. Her pathology revealed a three point seven centimeter poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. Interestingly, all of her margins were negative and the tumor itself was completely intrapancreatic. Nodes were negative, representing a T two and zero lesion.
